sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A cervical collar is an adjunct to spinal motion restriction when a cervical spine injury is suspected. It may help limit movement, but it does not by itself stabilise the neck and should never replace overall clinical judgement, manual support when needed, or time-critical care priorities.

sections.title: Sizing & Application
sections.content:
**Sizing:**

1. Size the collar according to the manufacturer's guide with the head in a neutral position if tolerated

2. Choose the closest appropriate size rather than forcing an awkward fit

**Application:**

1. Maintain manual spinal support while the collar is applied

2. Fit the collar according to device instructions, ensuring the chin is supported and the collar sits correctly on the shoulders and chest

3. Check that the collar does not worsen airway access, vomiting risk, distress, or obvious malposition

4. Reassess neurological status, comfort, and overall spinal packaging after application

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- A collar is only one part of spinal motion restriction and may be omitted or removed if it worsens the patient's airway, breathing, distress, or overall care
- Do not force the neck into a neutral position against resistance or obvious pain
- Cooperative patients may sometimes self-limit movement more effectively than a poorly tolerated collar
- Document the indication, size used, tolerance, and any reason the collar was modified, omitted, or removed
